result:
There is a specific kind of silence that belongs only to late autumn. It isn’t the heavy, muffled quiet of a deep snowfall, nor the dormant, buzzing hum of a summer afternoon. It is a brittle, expectant pause—the world holding its breath just before winter finally tightens its grip.

This morning, I walked through the local park just as the sun began to pale the sky from ink-black to bruised purple. The grass was stiff with frost, crunching faintly underfoot like crushed glass. Most of the leaves had already surrendered, leaving the oaks and maples to stand like exposed skeletal structures against the creeping dawn. The few that remained were brown and papery, clinging to their branches with a stubbornness that felt almost heroic. They rattled in the faint wind, a dry, percussive sound like a snare drum played with wire brushes.

I like this threshold season best. Summer is too loud, demanding endless outdoor evenings; spring is too anxious to please, bursting with frantic color. But autumn asks for nothing. It simply allows the year to wind down, stripping away the excess until only the essential architecture remains. There is a profound comfort in that kind of surrender. Watching a solitary leaf finally let go, spinning briefly before joining the rest on the frost-kissed earth, I felt a strange sense of permission. We, too, are allowed to let things drop.
